response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "As an older man who's curious about the world, I've been keeping up with technology through my part-time carpentry work and other hobbies like gardening. Social media is a bit overwhelming for me most times because there's so much noise online, including fake content that can be misleading or hurtful. But since I spend only a few times weekly on it anyway, I feel lucky to have developed some ways of handling this without getting too deep into the digital mess.

First thing first: don't go looking for trouble. Fake stuff isn't worth your time if you're not careful. When I'm checking my Facebook or Instagram feeds—usually just a quick look from my phone during coffee breaks—I skip anything that seems off, especially when it's about politics or sensational news. For example, last week someone shared an article with no source saying climate change is fake science; well, I read the title but didn't click further because that kind of thing feels like poison to common sense.

But if you see something interesting—like a recipe from baking or maybe a photo of my hiking friend—we should always verify it's real. That means checking for official sources whenever possible and being wary when someone claims expertise they don't have, such as making furniture without showing any work history online.

Also, I think everyone needs to set boundaries with social media because you can't control what others post anyway—especially since my kids sometimes share things on their pages that make me worry. So sticking to a schedule and ignoring the drama helps keep peace of mind.",
  "reasoning_summary": "I've been keeping up with technology through part-time carpentry work, so I'm curious about social media but not overly dependent. Since I spend only a few times weekly on it anyway, fake content can be managed by avoiding engagement with dubious posts and verifying information when necessary.

First step is to avoid looking at potentially harmful or unverified content—skip articles without sources or credible links—and stick to established news outlets where you can trace back to official statements. When something genuinely interesting comes up—like a recipe in baking or a photo from my hiking hobby—I fact-check it using reliable methods.

I also think boundaries matter, especially since I have kids who post things that might not be entirely truthful. So setting aside specific times for checking social media and then disconnecting helps me stay curious without getting dragged into fake news.",
  "tone": "Calm and pragmatic",
  "citations": [],
  "safety_notes": "None"
}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
As an older man who's curious about the world, I've been keeping up with technology through my part-time carpentry work and other hobbies like gardening. Social media is a bit overwhelming for me most times because there's so much noise online, including fake content that can be misleading or hurtful. But since I spend only a few times weekly on it anyway, I feel lucky to have developed some ways of handling this without getting too deep into the digital mess.

First thing first: don't go looking for trouble. Fake stuff isn't worth your time if you're not careful. When I'm checking my Facebook or Instagram feeds—usually just a quick look from my phone during coffee breaks—I skip anything that seems off, especially when it's about politics or sensational news. For example, last week someone shared an article with no source saying climate change is fake science; well, I read the title but didn't click further because that kind of thing feels like poison to common sense.

But if you see something interesting—like a recipe from baking or maybe a photo of my hiking friend—we should always verify it's real. That means checking for official sources whenever possible and being wary when someone claims expertise they don't have, such as making furniture without showing any work history online.

Also, I think everyone needs to set boundaries with social media because you can't control what others post anyway—especially since my kids sometimes share things on their pages that make me worry. So sticking to a schedule and ignoring the drama helps keep peace of mind.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
I've been keeping up with technology through part-time carpentry work, so I'm curious about social media but not overly dependent. Since I spend only a few times weekly on it anyway, fake content can be managed by avoiding engagement with dubious posts and verifying information when necessary.

First step is to avoid looking at potentially harmful or unverified content—skip articles without sources or credible links—and stick to established news outlets where you can trace back to official statements. When something genuinely interesting comes up—like a recipe in baking or a photo from my hiking hobby—I fact-check it using reliable methods.

I also think boundaries matter, especially since I have kids who post things that might not be entirely truthful. So setting aside specific times for checking social media and then disconnecting helps me stay curious without getting dragged into fake news.
